bio: Peter Saul (b. 1934, San Francisco, California) is an American painter known for his bold, satirical works that challenge politics, popular culture, and art history. He studied at the California School of Fine Arts in San Francisco and the Washington University School of Fine Arts in St. Louis. Over a career spanning more than six decades, Saul has been the subject of major retrospectives, including Peter Saul: Crime and Punishment at the New Museum in New York (2020) and Peter Saul: Pop, Funk, Bad Painting, and More at Les Abattoirs in Toulouse (2019). His work has been exhibited internationally and is held in the permanent collections of leading institutions, including the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Art Institute of Chicago, and the Centre Georges Pompidou. Saul has received numerous honors, including a Guggenheim Fellowship (1993), the Artist's Foundation Legacy Award (2008), and election to the American Academy of Arts and Letters (2010). He lives and works in New York City and Germantown, New York.
artists.name: Peter Saul

bio: Keith Haring (1958–1990) was an American artist whose bold, graphic style and iconic imagery became synonymous with New York City's downtown art scene of the 1980s. Born in Reading, Pennsylvania, he studied at the School of Visual Arts in New York, where he gained recognition for his chalk drawings in the city's subway stations. Haring's work explored themes of love, life, social justice, and activism, with a commitment to making art accessible through public murals, collaborations, and his Pop Shop. Throughout his career, he completed more than 50 public artworks worldwide and exhibited internationally. Following his AIDS diagnosis in 1988, Haring established the Keith Haring Foundation to support AIDS organizations and children's programs, using his art to promote awareness and advocacy. Today, his work remains a defining symbol of 20th-century contemporary art and is held in major museum collections around the world.
artists.name: Keith Haring

bio: Takashi Murakami (b. 1962, Tokyo, Japan) is one of the most influential contemporary Japanese artists, renowned for blending traditional Japanese art with anime, manga, pop culture, and commercial design. His vibrant paintings, sculptures, and installations explore themes of consumerism, history, and identity through recurring characters such as Mr. DOB, Kaikai, and Kiki. Murakami's work spans fine art, fashion, film, and design, reflecting his philosophy of dissolving the boundaries between high and low culture. His work has been exhibited internationally at major museums and galleries, and he continues to shape contemporary art through both his artistic practice and his production company, Kaikai Kiki.
artists.name: Takashi Murakami

bio: Godley Brown is the New York–based artist duo of Catherine Walsh and Marcarson, whose multidisciplinary practice transforms found and discarded materials into sculptural works, furniture, collage, and fashion. Drawing from Walsh's background in ballet and Marcarson's experience in painting and design, the duo creates tactile, immersive works that blur the boundaries between art and everyday objects. Their practice explores materiality, reuse, and craftsmanship, reimagining overlooked textiles and industrial materials into richly textured compositions that challenge conventional ideas of beauty, function, and value.
artists.name: Godley Brown

bio:
Michel Friess (b. 1970) in Neustadt an der Weinstrasse in Germany. At an early age he was attracted to street art and was later able to make a name for himself under his label “GERMAN POP ART”.
The artist attaches importance to complete handwork. With his mixed technique of painting, airbrush with spray cans, hand-print, and paper collages, works are created in which the dedication and attention to detail are clearly visible and perceptible in each of his works.
His work has received international attention and is in some of the most important collections around the world. Friess has also been honored with many art awards and is represented by more than 30 galleries throughout Germany plus galleries in Shanghai, Austria, Spain, and now New York City.
artists.born: b. 1970, Neustadt an der Weinstrasse, Germany
artists.name: Michel Friess

bio: Alex Silver is an American photographer whose work explores the divine feminine through intimate, fine art portraiture that emphasizes elegance, grace, and emotional connection. A graduate of NYU Tisch School of the Arts, his photographs have been exhibited internationally in Paris, London, New York, Miami, Rome, Athens, and Glasgow, and are held in private collections. After studying fashion in Rome in 2023, Silver expanded his practice into editorial and commercial photography. Based in Colorado, he works internationally and is also the founder of Equilibrium Retreats, a wellness initiative focused on sustainable, off-grid retreats that promote holistic living and harmony with nature.
artists.name: Alex Silver
TJ Roszko
bio: TJ Roszko (b. 2002, New York) is an American contemporary artist whose practice explores mental health, identity, and the complexities of human experience. Working through large-scale abstraction, he builds his paintings through layering, erasure, and reconstruction, creating compositions that balance order and disruption while seeking tranquility within chaos. Living with obsessive-compulsive disorder, Roszko uses painting to challenge rigidity and embrace uncertainty, transforming instinctive gestures into acts of acceptance and possibility. His exploration of sexuality and identity further informs the layered nature of his work, reflecting the evolving and often unknowable dimensions of the self.

artists.name: Cathryn Czudej
bio:
Catharine Czudej (Johannesburg, South Africa, 1985) lives and works in New York, USA. She holds a BFA from from New York University, New York and a MFA from University of California, Los Angeles. 
Czudej’s work has been the subject of international exhibitions, most recently at von ammon co, Washington DC; Benevento, Los Angeles; Kölnischer Kunstverein, Cologne and Office Baroque, Brussels. Institutional presentations include Fürstenberg Zeitgenössisch, Donaueschingen; Arsenal, Toronto and Irvine Fine Arts Center, Irvine, among others.

artists.name: Michael Hafftka
bio: Michael Hafftka (b. 1953, New York City) is an American painter whose deeply expressive work explores memory, history, spirituality, and the human condition. Born to Holocaust survivor parents, Hafftka's personal history has profoundly shaped his artistic practice. His work is held in the permanent collections of major institutions including the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Museum of Modern Art, the Brooklyn Museum, the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, the Carnegie Museum of Art, and the National Gallery of Art. His career has been marked by major solo exhibitions and retrospectives, including presentations at the Museum of Modern Art, the Housatonic Museum of Art, and the Yeshiva University Museum. In recent years, Hafftka has also become a leading figure in the digital art community while continuing to collaborate with poets and writers on interdisciplinary projects.

artists.name: Ryszard Semko
bio:
Ryszard Semko's artistic journey has been a profound exploration of his inner self, shaped by personal hardship and the life-changing experience of relocating to New York City from Poland as a political refugee. This new reality brought feelings of isolation and nostalgia, which he confronted and explored through his art. His early focus on self-portraits became an ongoing dialogue with his inner emotions, with each painting reflecting his psychological and emotional state. By 2010, however, he found himself mentally and emotionally exhausted, leading him to step away from portraiture.

In 2012, Semko turned to monotype printmaking, creating unique landscapes that opened new possibilities for intuitive self-expression. As he developed this practice, he embraced the freedom and spontaneity of the medium. In 2023, he returned to self-portraiture with a new series that revisited the shadows of his past. Through these works, he discovered that certain experiences remain permanently etched into his life, serving as enduring reminders of the defining moments that continue to shape his artistic vision.

artists.name: Jean-Michel Basquiat
bio: Jean-Michel Basquiat (1960–1988) was an American artist born in Brooklyn to Haitian and Puerto Rican parents. Emerging from New York City's graffiti scene under the tag SAMO, he rose to prominence in the early 1980s with expressive paintings that fused text, symbols, and imagery inspired by jazz, history, literature, and popular culture. Throughout his career, Basquiat confronted racism, inequality, and the art world's commodification of Black artists while celebrating Black history, identity, and cultural traditions. Before his death at age twenty-seven, he produced an extraordinary body of work whose layered compositions and powerful social commentary continue to shape contemporary art and conversations surrounding race, identity, and power.

artists.name: Vitaliy Frankiv
bio:
Vitaliy Frankiv is a Ukrainian American abstract artist based in New York City. His artistic journey began with graffiti and urban exploration during his early teenage years. Those formative experiences offered him a glimpse of what it meant to be not only an artist, but also a sovereign human being. Experimenting with spray paint, bucket paint, and breathtaking landscapes profoundly shaped his creative path. Above all, it was color that captivated him. The first time he combined spray paint with canvas, he experienced a creative awakening. He could spend hours tracing every color across a painting, observing how each hue flowed into another to create something far greater than the sum of its parts.

Frankiv creates original abstract paintings defined by distinctive color combinations, layered patterns, and rich textures. Every work is entirely unique and cannot be replicated; no two paintings are ever identical. This individuality lies at the heart of his practice. Every splash of paint is intentional, deeply personal, and thoughtfully placed. The longer a viewer engages with his work, the more its complexity reveals itself. What may first appear chaotic gradually transforms into something harmonious, vibrant, mystical, and expressive. He is particularly drawn to pairing colors that might traditionally seem incompatible, bringing them together in unexpected balance.

At the core of Frankiv's practice is the desire to create paintings that are cherished, respected, and celebrated around the world—not only in galleries or on empty walls, but within homes where they become meaningful parts of everyday life. His works are intended to be more than decorative objects; they serve as lasting reminders of personal moments, emotions, and connections. Collecting one of his paintings is an investment not only in art, but also in one's space, perspective, and relationship to the energy embodied within each piece. His goal is to enrich both the environments his paintings inhabit and the lives of those who live with them. Seeing his work become part of someone's daily experience, extending beyond aesthetics into personal meaning, brings him profound purpose and joy.

For those who feel an immediate connection to one of his paintings, Frankiv believes that instinct should be trusted. If a piece speaks to someone, it has already found its home.
